"Comparative Challenges: Homer and Theocritus into Latin, Virgil into Greek." Mediterranean Chronicle 7: 227-34. Romani Mistretta, Marco. 2017. "Le Virgile français: Jacques Delille's Commented Edition of the Aeneid." IJCT 24: 175-97. Frick considers the first translation of the Aeneid into German, by Thomas Murner (Strasbourg, 1515); and the later translation by Johannes Spreng, published posthumously in 1610. Heuzé discusses challenges encountered by translators of the Aeneid. After noting the influence of Johann Heinrich Voß's translation of Homer on metrical translations of Latin poetry, Holzberg argues for the possibility of a metrical translation of the Aeneid that avoids artificial and archaizing language. Paschalis discusses Renaissance humanists' translations of Vergil's Eclogues into Doric Greek hexameters, imitating the poetic style of Theocritus; and their translations of Theocritus into Latin prose or, less commonly, into Vergilian-style Latin hexameters. Homer was also translated into Vergilian hexameters. Romani Mistretta discusses the Latin text, French translation, notes, and illustrations of the Aeneid edited by Jacques Delille (1738-1813). Kayachev offers a host of emendations in Ciris: at 68, he proposes ficto for the transmitted toto; after 73, suggests adding a line such as tu, pater, o Neptune pati male dignior illa and at 73 emending uiolauerat to uiolaueras; at 79 reads maris instead of malis; emends 86 to animos meretrix induta ferarum; proposes formosam at 90, comparing 487-89; reads candida caesaries florebant tempora circum at 121; suggests introducing a verb (uolet or uolat) at 204; at 264-65 proposes reading eheu, | qui dicam quoue ausa malum hoc exordiar ore?; and at 301, corrects obnixe to obnixi, taking this word as an adjective. At Dirae 15 Kayachev proposes reading effetae Cererem sulcis condatis harenae, "may you bury wheat in furrows of sterile sand." In composing his Cento nuptialis, writes Budaragina, Ausonius allows
himself to borrow a maximum of one and a half contiguous lines from Vergil's
text. The text at praef. 26-27 is thus seriously corrupt and should be marked
with a crux: "unus † et sequens cum medio …". The Cento Tityri edited by
Canellis is an erudite Christian eclogue based on Vergil's verse, put in the
form of a dialogue and drawing on various genres. Clément-Tarantino and
Klein write that the cento Europa uses fragments of Vergil's verse but derives
its subject matter from Ovid, Metam. 2. It thus paradoxically represents a
Vergilian rereading of Ovid. Fels and Greschat offer a translation of and
commentary on Proba's Cento. Kochenash suggests that the mention of
Tabitha at Acts 9.36-43 immediately after Peter's healing of Aeneas can be
read as an allusion to Vergil's Dido, since Aramaic Tabitha and Greek Dorcas
(the translation of her name) both mean "deer" and Dido is characterized
as a deer in the Aeneid. Marin (2017b) observes that Jerome, Epist. 30.13.2,
alludes to Georg. 2.506. Tautschnig discusses Seneca's allusion in his Epist.
21 to the Nisus and Euryalus episode. Vallat analyzes a database of Vergilian
centones to discover which passages were best known in late antiquity, and
offers in an appendix a list of the Vergilian verses cited three or more times.
Wasyl's edition includes the Latin text of two poems preserved in Paris,
Bibliothèque nationale de France, MS lat. 10318. The cento Alcestis, inspired
by Vergil, bears witness to literary production in North Africa under the
Vandals.
